speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead. Let's slate it, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 16, 1995. I am interviewing Wilhelmina De Groot. W-I-L-H-E-L-M-I-N-A. Last name is D-E, capital G-R-O-O-T, De Groot. My name is Stella-- S-T-E-L-L-A-- Eliezrie-- E-L-I-E-Z-R-I-E. We're in Murietta Hot Springs, California, in America. And we will be speaking in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon. Today we're interviewing Wilhelmina De Groot. Could you please tell us what your name was at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wilhelmina Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what your parents' names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Clara Diamond. And my father was Samuel Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were you in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. Two boys, one girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My eldest brother was Albert. My youngest brother was Jack, and I-- I was in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 22nd, 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: That makes you 80--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: 84-- Could you tell us where did you live in-- were you in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first-- well, I remember the first one was Paddington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a suburb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, it's really London. It's not-- it's a couple of miles out of, out of West End.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a cellist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a musician, a cellist--

speaker: interviewee
text: A musician. And he worked in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he perform?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the musical comedies in the hippodrome in Piccadilly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he receive his musical training?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Antwerp and in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he born in England as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- no. He was born in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amsterdam, Holland. And your mother, what country what she from originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also from Holland, also born in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents when you were growing up as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother's parents were alive. Her name was Wilhelmina Cumerman And my grandfather was Joseph Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what they did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my, my grandfather was in-- a diamond cutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, like--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandmother was a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father played, performed music. Did he travel a great deal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He traveled when he was younger. He lived in Paris first, because his-- should I go on with his elder brother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: His elder brother was born a genius. He was a violinist. He was a conductor of 100 men in Paris Orchestra when he was four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's unheard of. Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have you heard of? He was compared to Jascha Heifetz. They were compared to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about, as a child growing up in your own home, your mother, what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She just looked after us three kids. Looked-- a lot of looking after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in an apartment or in a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in an apartment above an office building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- would you-- how would you describe your family financially, their economic situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle, middle class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father expect the children in your home to learn music as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My two, my two brothers were brought up from four years old to be musicians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which instruments were those that they played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Violin and trumpet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was on piano.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy playing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wanted to be a dancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did. You pursued that also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I went to dancing school in Kensington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your education as a child. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school-- in private schools, kindergartens, of course, in London. And then we moved to Southend-on-Sea, where I went to Southend Grammar School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after grammar school, where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After grammar school, I went to Rottingdean. That's near Brighton. I was-- went to a convent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that a Jewish child was sent to a convent school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my, my father's brother married a Christian woman, and she had a, a daughter. And this daughter went to that school. And she persuaded my mom and my dad to send me there, because it was a very good education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism there as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. I was-- as a matter of fact, I was a favorite in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know that you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that didn't interfere in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did your family observe Judaism in any way in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by eating no lobsters and no rabbit, nothing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend a synagogue as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother light Shabbos candles when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And holidays-- Passover or Hanukkah-- do you remember those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well they always-- they knew. And we had special treats on those days, but not to say-- it was a tradition in the family. It's tradition of the Jewish people that they always observe something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like this-- I have for luck, somebody gave me this. I think I must have been about 14, and I can't remember who. And I've had it ever since. And wherever I go, this is what-- my husband doesn't even-- it's a secret. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your-- a secret your husband does not know about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he doesn't know. Maybe he did once know, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you hold that up next to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --he forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold that up next to your face. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: It should be like that, you know? It broke off. [INAUDIBLE] years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what it is?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, tell me what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put it on the door for, for-- Jewish people have it always on the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a mezuzah case? That's called a mezuzah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a friend of mine years and years-- I must have been about 13 or 14. And she said, I want you to keep this always with you because this is your guardian angel. And it never left me. I always had it. It was whole once, but it broke off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the significance of that, of that mezuzah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, but I know it brings-- I know it keeps you safe and it brings you luck. Of course, in my life, whatever I did, I've always fallen on my feet the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you have to fall--

speaker: interviewee
text: So it must be something.

speaker: interviewer
text: I heard a great expression. If you have to fall, you might as well fall on your feet and not on your face.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good way to fall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good way to fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you keep that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always-- my purse.

speaker: interviewer
text: You keep it on your body all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You keep it nearby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Always-- it's always with me in my purse. Every purse I take, I change it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you shown it to Louis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I-- he may not even know. He may know. I don't know if he knows. He doesn't remember things so well, and he hasn't seen it for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. That's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's nothing really I keep secret from him. It's just something that just happens, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's yours. It's a-- personal. It's your history.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's personal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, it is. After high-- tell me about your childhood, as far as your hobbies.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hobbies as a child? What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I used to do pen painting on black velvet. It's like a pen with special thick ink. And you pencil flowers on velvet, all sorts of decorations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And dancing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hang the pictures on a frame? Is it something that would go on the wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were decorations. You made bracelets, and necklaces, and things like that. And cushions. You can make whole cushions with it too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your dancing, tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dancing. I went to a dancing school in Kensington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A professional one. A professional dancing school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you studied that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 14, 15, between the ages of that and 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents pleased with your choice to dance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My, my, my mother, my mother had everything to say. My father listens. [LAUGHTER] They were very, very good couple. They were always called Muff and Jeff. I never heard them have a quarrel or a bad word, in all the, in all the times we lived-- I lived with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's almost unheard of nowadays.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is. [LAUGHS] He was a very, very gentle man. He was also a gentleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was very friendly with everybody, and she gave good advice. I give-- that same advice, I give to my children and to my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the disciplinarian between the two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother-- my father had nothing to do with the children. He just worked and brought in a salary. You know how it was in those days. The man worked and the woman worked in the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got, he got his pocket money for cigarettes, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was enough for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother paid all the bills, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what did you study when you were learning dance? What kind of dance was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Acrobatic and ballet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you study for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite a long time, but my father was a very, very old fashioned man. I was-- I was never allowed to take a job on the stage. He just wanted me to learn, because I wanted to learn that. But he would never let me take a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was against it, because he was-- he saw what was going on in the arts world, like it is today.

speaker: interviewer
text: He felt it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's worse today than it was then. Because he always used to say, if you want to get on in the film business or in the stage business, you have to go to bed the director. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the case then as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only way to get on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was concerned about modesty and about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Integrity. Was that frustrating not being able to perform publicly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, yes. But I used to do it for friends, and you know, parties friends gave, and things like that. I used to do it privately. He didn't mind about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your other interests? Did you have many friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had a lot-- we had lots of friends. But I was never allowed out alone with friends. I was taken to a party, I was taken back from a party.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the custom of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly, but a lot of my friends were much freer than I was. I was the Prisoner of Zenda. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers used to call me that. And what you attribute that over-protectiveness to? Was it his-- was it your family's upbringing? Was it something that their parents had done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's the way they were brought up too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever rebel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I didn't know what-- we kids in those days, we didn't know what that was, rebelling against your parents. Your parents were your gods. Everything they did, everything they said, was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your friends? Were there Jewish children among the kids that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There were Jewish children too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish youth organizations in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know that now. You know how long ago that is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you attend a college after school, after dance school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The grammar school, the grammar school was the last school I attended. It was a-- it was a very, very good school, well known for, for its-- the teaching high-class things, more than a public school. They were advanced.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was something that your parents paid for privately.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- at what age did you go-- at what age did you finish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I was around about 16. Then I went into another school to teach the kindergarten for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was good. I enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Mayvale.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a suburb of London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to become a kindergarten teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother-- my mother said you should do that, so I did it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all. Did you like working with the small children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I liked that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did that for a while--

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while, and then I went to the dancing school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after dancing school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After dancing school, I wanted to learn hairdressing. So I went in for hairdressing for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in Regent Street in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you enjoy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a more appropriate choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very happy youth. I enjoyed everything I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother and sister get along with you well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I got along better was my eldest brother than my youngest brother. He was born on my first birthday. He was my birthday present. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to London, did you live in the city? Were you still living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. This is all a suburb. I went with the underground home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued to live at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about 20 minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you finished studying to be a hairdresser, what did you do with those skills?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I studied to be a hairdresser, I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell us about how you found your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- my, my grandparents lived in Antwerp. And my mother said, you have to go and see your grandparents. They're getting older. I never did want to go to, to see them. I didn't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you seen them at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw them once or twice when I was little, because they went to live in South Africa. But when they came back from South Africa, they lived in Antwerp again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, why did you-- why did they travel to South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diamonds. My grandfather was in the diamond business. Diamond cutter, diamonds-- everything in diamonds, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which city were they in in South Africa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Johannesburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I still have family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents, though, never moved there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went there. We went there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you? When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --once. That was when I was about-- you see that photo, the photo there? I was about-- I was about three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been a very long journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was in those days, I suppose.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't notice it, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a child. When you decided-- when your parents decided it was time to go see your grandparents, what did you-- were you pleased to go see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was-- you know, every kid is pleased with thinking, of liking to go to another country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 18, 19. I took my youngest brother with me, because I wasn't allowed to travel alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us how you met your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I met my husband, we were in Antwerp to see my grandparents. And my cousin was engaged to Louis' brother. So she said, you know, I have to go Friday evening to my parents-in-law for dinner. And what are you going to do? And she said, I would like you to go with me. I said, I'll go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with her to see her parents at dinner, and I saw Louis coming out of a shoemaker shop. So she said, oh that's my fiance's brother. But he was only about 13 at that time, so I didn't take any notice of him, of course. And that's, that's the first time I saw him. And how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh. So you didn't get married then.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I got-- I saw him once before when a few years younger, when he was 13 and I was 15. But I didn't-- it wasn't anything special. I just didn't take any notice. He was so young, you know? He was a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this time, when you went to, to see your, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents? That time?

speaker: interviewer
text: --grandmother, what happened differently this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, I got engaged to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he took me out at evening. He, he had a girlfriend, so we went both together to say to her he couldn't go, because he had friends from England. So he took-- we went out dancing. And he was-- I didn't understand him, he didn't understand me. But we danced. And you know, I don't know how it happened. But in two days time, he asked me to marry him. And I was sleeping with an aunt at my grandparents' place, and they brought me a bowl of flowers up.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were old flowers that they had downstairs. I said, what's this in honor of? She said, well, you got engaged to Louis De Groot last night. I said, I did not. She said, oh yes, you did. So the-- I was so shy, I didn't even dare to say that's not true. So I said, oh, that's possible. So that's the way it remained. And we got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] It's silly. I won a-- I won a prize on television with this story once too, when we first came to Canada thirty years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you concerned that you didn't know what this man was talking about? You didn't understand his language?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was so naive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a real child. When I was 17, I didn't even know what-- when a woman was pregnant. When she was this big, I didn't even know it was that. I thought it was a sickness. I was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it about Louis that made you fall in love with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a nice guy. He's awfully nice to me. He was a nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he handsome?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hadn't ever been out with guys or anything like that, you know. So I fell in love with him, I suppose. If that's love like that, it's love at first sight. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned that you would marry a person from a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't ever even thought of anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents have been upset if he was from a non-Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. My husband-- my parents were very happy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you would have said that you were bringing home someone who was not Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they wouldn't have liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They always wanted me to marry a Jewish guy, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Louis' family became--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Your parents-in-law. Now, what happened after you became engaged? Then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think for a week or two. And then Louis came over to ask my dad my hand. You know how that goes years ago?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us how it worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to come and ask for your-- for the girl's hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband? Yes. He was scared, because he tore a pound note in half in his anxiety. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your parents' impression of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they thought he was a little bit crazy, tearing money in half. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they liked him. They liked him. He was a nice guy. He was polite.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they concerned about you moving to another country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they want you to stay nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- they did really want me to stay by, but things don't go that way. They saw we were going to do it, and that was it. My father was a very sick man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he sick with?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- my mother would never tell us what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I guess it was cancer on the liver.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: She would never tell us that but, you know, years ago, the word cancer wasn't spoken. That was talked of as an illness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did he die at an early age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: From cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the wedding ceremony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that wasn't too much. We got married in a, in a, in the synagogue. It wasn't too much--

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --fuss about it. It was a small one, I don't remember-- in Antwerp. That was in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A rabbi officiated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, the next day, that same rabbi ran away with a young girl. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I shouldn't have asked about the rabbi. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's so much for the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, well. What is it a-- OK. Let's skip the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went home to my mother-in-law's house. My mother was there. My father was very sick, so he couldn't come over. He had to stay in England. My mother came for a few days, and my mother-in-law made dinner. And we had a few friends over in the house. And that was our wedding day party.

speaker: interviewer
text: That sounds very pleasant. What did you wear at your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Black-- black velvet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I like black velvet, and I didn't particularly want to get married in white.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't it the general custom to get married in white?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody got married in white, as far as I know. But I liked black.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people surprised to see you in that dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't really think anybody in those days took any notice of things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, my mother-in-law didn't think it was bad or anything like that. She was, she was brought up religiously, but she didn't say anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- you told me before that you were married by the glove.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you explain to us what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is, my mother was in Antwerp, and my father lived in Paris before they were married. And then my father went to London. And my grandparents wouldn't let my mother go over without being married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's just a sort of a prayer that's made, when my father has a glove in his hand in London, and my mother has a glove in her hand in Antwerp. So but you were-- you are legitimate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. 84 Years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely so. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you receive gifts when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not, not too many. I think I had an old sewing machine from my mother-in-law. And I think I had a glass with, a water glass with six cups from one of my brothers. And from my mother, she, she gave me a house in Antwerp, which she had there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was generous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from friends, a fishbowl was some fish running around in it. And from another friend, a table. Those sort of things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your plan after you got married? Where did you intend to live then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Antwerp. We lived in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in the apartment that your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the little house, we lived. We-- oh, no. First, we lived in-- first we lived in a little flat. And there, there, Ronnie was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you were married was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine and a half months. Nine and a half months. Everybody wants to make it eight and a half, but it was nine and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy to have had a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you want to have a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did want to have a big family, but when the war broke out, we decided not to bring anymore kids into the world, in case something like this could happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed with two boys, and then we adopted a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, we adopted a girl, but was 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- she was-- she had an aunt and uncle living around the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which-- where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ixelles, where we lived in Ixelles during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Antwerp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was-- her aunt and her uncle-- she, she was-- started to do things, and write secret letters. And, and we couldn't manage her. She was 15. And she was jealous of the boys. And I treated her better than I treated my own children, because she was adopted. And then we just said-- then the aunt and uncle decided that we'd better send her to them. And she wanted to go, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a Jewish child?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was Jewish-- Polish Jewish child. And she brought up, you know, different to our bringing up. And she was better off with her aunt and her uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her parents were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted-- really, we wanted to adopt a baby girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't work that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't work that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your children have Jewish names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Ronald is not a Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack, Jack, Ronald--

speaker: interviewer
text: At their bris, were they given a Jewish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them had circumcisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ronnie is just Ronald. And Jack is Jack Richard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they named after someone in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a silly thing to say, but my eldest son was named after a film star, which which I was crazy about. I said, if I have a son, he's going to be named Ronald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he named for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ronald Colman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you ever heard of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I have. [LAUGHS] And your second son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That second son is, is named after my brother-- after an uncle and after my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had happened to her brother? Was your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, you shouldn't do that, but I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Sephardim have such a custom.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was very proud to have him named, named after him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I have a grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop. We're going to stop. OK.
